children.text: Can you tell me how as a woman, as a Chicana, you were treated differently than other Brown Berets?

Well, I wasn't treated differently by some of the members. The other, I mean you know. There were some expectations, like I said, even some of our west side Berets, because they believed the women should be at home, and making supper, and raising the kids, and putting up with everybody’s shit, you know. I mean, you know. Those were just like, basically individual attitudes, right? Overall, generally, among the west side Berets because that is my initial experience- and you know I wasn't around long enough to tell you about the south side. But pretty much they accepted me. And I went through the training, and I had to deal with all of that- those sometimes attitudes. But mostly I went through all the training. I learned how to dismantle rifles and put 'em together, to load 'em and unload 'em. We went through self-defense training. We had our prime minister of self-defense, and I still talk to him. He's a minister by the way. He preaches. So, anyway we went through the whole- it was like military training man. It was just the whole thing, you know. I was pretty physically fit at that time, you know. It was just incredible. We were expected to march, and you know just like along side them. And there's several incidents you know- and my compadre can probably tell you about that- you know when we were in the valley and had several confrontations that- And we came this close (holds up index finger and thumb close together) to- to actually having it out with law enforcement.
Can you tell us about one of those instances?

Yeah. And I can't- I can't tell you the particulars because I don'tt remember too much. All I remember is I was loading and passing out rifles, and loading and passing out rifles. And it was the whole block. We had the whole block. And I was going to get it, in other words, because I was in the van passing these things out. And we had whole – we had the van loaded with rifles. And I remember coming back we got stopped by the immigration check point and they opened- (laughter) they opened the van, and we're sitting there (laughter). And the guy just closes the van and says "go on." You know he didn't even- (laughter) He didn't even ask "are you United States citizens?" He didn't even anything, just like “get going.” And it was so funny you know, when we were coming back. But when we were there I actually thought we were gonna have a- a shoot out. You know, “we're going to shoot it out here with the- with the-” And that was my job to load up those rifles and keep them coming, you know. So I loaded them all. And I knew how to do it, I mean they taught me how to do it. And we were doing it like in record time, man. Vamanos. So...
What were some of the protests you did in San Antonio with the Berets?

We protested Mayor McAllister. No, you don't want names, right? (Interviewer says "No, no" as Montejano continues speaking) We protested Mayor McAllister and his savings in loan thing and fiasco. His comments about “you give them beer and mariachis and tamales and they will vote for you.” That protest. We protested police brutality. That was our number one focus, the police brutality. We joined with the Cesar Chavez marcha and we went to Austin, you know with that marcha over there, where they raised the- Have you talked to Jorge Valasquez? Where raised the Mexican flag over the capital of Texas. And I'll never forget that because you know how they have their little basement, or whatever, all the police in Austin were there in their riot gear and we were looking at them, you know, because that was one the things. We were supposed to be the protectors of the marchers, you know? And so we passed on the word that those guys are over there, you know, just waiting, just waiting. You know, and I'll never forget. And I don't know who has the pictures, because I know that people took pictures of us raising the Mexican flag over- over the capital building, man. That was just so awesome, man. That was just something else, but they didn't say anything, you know. Mainly, it was the marchas for the farm workers, the equal rights, the equal pay, education, more educational opportunities, and of course the police brutality. And at that time, I'm sure you read about they killed that 13 year old boy in Dallas. Yeah, they shot him under the house. So, that was- that was one of our- and a lot of the kids on the west side were getting beat up, you know, so... BY the police.

United States Immigration Check Points are locations on roads within the United States where Customs and Border Patrol (CBP) agents are legally allowed to stop cars, question drivers and passengers on citizenship status, and inspect vehicles. They can be located anywhere within 100 miles of the border. Many are located in Texas on the roads that connect towns in the Rio Grande Valley to other cities further inland, like San Antonio. Drivers and passengers are not required to respond to questions, but CBP officers do not need a warrant to search the vehicle if they feel they have resonable suspicion.

Walter W. McAllister served as Mayor of San Antonio from 1961 to 1971. He was mayor during the controversial HemisFair '68 and was piviotal to the development which claimed the Mexican American neighborhood previously present on the land. "McAllister Freeway," a section of Highway 281, was built in in his honor.
